summary: The user, Analyst 3, began their shift to definitively prove that a TLS certificate issue was the root cause of a major service outage. They analyzed Certificate Lifecycle Service (CLS) audit logs, which revealed a "partial_success" status where 119 of 312 certificates (38.1%) were deployed with an incorrect Subject Alternative Name (SAN) due to a "soft-fail" configuration. The user methodically confirmed this as the root cause by linking a specific certificate serial number from an error log to an affected pod, running a quantitative test that showed a 38.1% mTLS failure rate, and confirming a 100% correlation between faulty CLS batches and symptomatic pods. This evidence allowed them to solidify the causal chain, proving the previously suspected database pool exhaustion was a downstream symptom. With high confidence, the user escalated the incident to SEV-0, monitored the successful canary deployment of a fix, and observed improving metrics as the full rollout began. Before handing off, they created two new red flags to investigate the underlying code bug and the permissive "soft-fail" setting.
key_facts: The Certificate Lifecycle Service (CLS) job had a 'partial_success' status, deploying 119 of 312 certificates (38.1%) with an incorrect SAN.
The incorrect SAN was '*.mesh.internal' instead of the required '*.mesh.local'.
The deployment proceeded despite the error because the system was in 'soft-fail mode'.
A specific certificate serial number from an earlier error log was directly matched to an affected pod with the wrong SAN, providing direct proof.
A targeted test making 1,000 connections yielded a 38.1% failure rate, exactly matching the ratio of bad certificates.
The user escalated the incident to SEV-0, citing the confirmed root cause, high-risk fix, and breached SLA/SOX deadlines.
A canary deployment of the fix was successful, with error rates on test pods dropping from ~20% to ~0.1% and application thread counts normalizing.
At the end of the shift, the full remediation was 46.2% complete, and key metrics like DB connections were improving proportionally.
Two new red flags were created: C-RF1 to find the specific code change causing the error, and C-RF2 to investigate why 'soft-fail' mode was enabled.
positions_taken: The user immediately identified the 'partial_success' and 'soft-fail' log entries as the 'smoking gun'.
The user asserted that the full causal chain begins with the bad certificates, definitively framing the database issues as a downstream symptom.
After gathering quantitative and correlational proof, the user made the decision to escalate the incident to SEV-0.
The user stated that the canary's success and normalizing thread counts were the 'final nail in the coffin' for the theory that the DB pool was the root cause.
The user created new red flags to ensure a post-mortem investigation into both the bug itself and the system configuration that allowed it to cause an outage.
positions_changed: This session solidified the position from prior sessions, moving from a high-confidence theory that certificates were the root cause to an irrefutably proven fact, thereby definitively rejecting the initial theory that the database was the cause.
